summary: Addresses external review #7 feedback: added empty-string guard before cron.ParseStandard in validate() to produce a clear field.Required error instead of a cryptic parser message, added a dedicated unit test for the empty RotationSchedule path, and documented two new review patterns capturing the lessons learned.

tests_checklist.item: Tests exist and pass (make test OPERATOR=keystone: all green)
Edge cases covered (empty string, invalid cron, valid cron all tested)
New test TestValidate_EmptyRotationScheduleReturnsRequiredError verifies Required error type
Existing TestValidate_InvalidCronExpressions/empty still passes (now hits Required path instead of parse error)

code_quality_checklist.item: Follows existing code patterns (if/else if guard pattern, field.Required/field.Invalid usage)
Comment explains design rationale (CRD marker pipeline vs webhook defaulting)
Minimal change — only the lines needed to fix the issue

security_checklist.item: All user inputs validated (RotationSchedule now validated for both empty and invalid cases)
Defense-in-depth maintained (webhook guard complements CRD schema default marker)

architecture_checklist.item: Solution is as simple as possible (single if/else if guard)
No scope creep — changes strictly address external review feedback

fail_fast_checklist.item: Empty RotationSchedule now fails fast with actionable error before reaching parser

defensive_checklist.item: Empty-string guard prevents cryptic cron parser errors when called outside admission pipeline

suggested_improvements: Minor: TestValidate_InvalidCronExpressions includes '' (empty) as a test case at line 229, which now hits the field.Required path instead of the cron parse error path. The subtest name 'empty' is still accurate but the test group name 'InvalidCronExpressions' is semantically imprecise for this case. Consider moving the empty-string case out of the table-driven invalid cron test since it now has its own dedicated test (TestValidate_EmptyRotationScheduleReturnsRequiredError). Low priority — no functional impact.
